Activity Description: Choose a visual text. List 15 literal observations without interpretation (colours, sizes, positions, people, objects). No inference allowed. Timer: 3 minutes.
Support Level: Highly Scaffolded — steps are laid out for you
Why It Helps: Sharpens the fast, accurate first read that Paper 1 rewards — seeing what a text is doing before you write a word.
Success Criteria: You can state the text's purpose, audience, and one key technique without rereading.
If You Are Stuck: Cover the text. Ask only: who made this, for whom, and what do they want me to feel? Then look again.

Activity Description: Take this vague thesis: 'The advertisement uses many techniques to persuade the audience.' Now upgrade it to the formula in three rounds: Round 1 — add a specific feature. Round 2 — add a specific purpose phrase.

Activity Description: Pick any pair of Blake poems with the same title (e.g. 'The Chimney Sweeper' Innocence and Experience). Write one sentence describing what stays the same between the two poems and one sentence describing what changes.

Success Criteria: You can connect this poem's technique to your global issue in one spoken sentence.
If You Are Stuck: Say the poem's title and finish aloud: 'This poem is really about…' Don't overthink — your gut reading is a fine starting point.

Activity Description: Gallenkuş places two photographs, drawn from radically different social and economic contexts, in a single composite image. The line between them — the literal threshold between two worlds — is the work's central argument.
Why It Helps: Builds your Gallenkuş knowledge for the IO — split-frame juxtapositions that make global inequality impossible to unsee.
Success Criteria: You can explain how the two halves of one composite speak to each other.
If You Are Stuck: Cover one half of the image. React to the visible half. Then reveal — the jolt you feel IS the technique.

Activity Description: Every text has three possible audiences: the primary (the intended target), the secondary (those who encounter it incidentally), and the tertiary (those who study or critique it). For any text, identify all three layers.
Why It Helps: Sharpens the fast, accurate first read that Paper 1 rewards — seeing what a text is doing before you write a word.
Success Criteria: You can state the text's purpose, audience, and one key technique without rereading.
If You Are Stuck: Cover the text. Ask only: who made this, for whom, and what do they want me to feel? Then look again.

Activity Description: Build a personal tonal vocabulary bank. Collect 20 tone/mood words (beyond 'sad' and 'happy'): wistful, sardonic, elegiac, didactic, menacing, conspiratorial, reverent, sycophantic, acerbic, valedictory.

Activity Description: Find any sentence from any text you are studying. Swap one key word for a neutral synonym. How does the swap change the sentence's tone, argument, and effect? The gap between the original word and the neutral swap is where the analysis lives.
Why It Helps: Precise technique-spotting with effects is what moves Criterion B from 'describes' to 'analyses'.
Success Criteria: You can name the technique, quote the exact words, and state the effect in one sentence.
If You Are Stuck: Find the one word or image you'd never use in ordinary speech. That's usually the technique.

Support Level: Highly Scaffolded — steps are laid out for you
Why It Helps: Paper 1 hands you an unfamiliar text type — knowing each type's conventions means you start analysing instead of decoding.
Success Criteria: You can name two conventions of this text type and how this example uses or bends them.
If You Are Stuck: List what you'd EXPECT this text type to do. Then hunt for the one place this example does something different.

Activity Description: If appeals persuades, identify: How is credibility established (Ethos)? What evidence/logic is used (Logos)? What emotional appeals appear (Pathos)? Which dominates? Create pie chart showing rhetorical appeal distribution. How effective is this combination for the intended audience? What appeals are absent?
Why It Helps: Paper 1 hands you an unfamiliar text type — knowing each type's conventions means you start analysing instead of decoding.
Success Criteria: You can name two conventions of this text type and how this example uses or bends them.
If You Are Stuck: List what you'd EXPECT this text type to do. Then hunt for the one place this example does something different.

Activity Description: For appeals with visual component: does text anchor (explain/limit) image meaning or does image expand/subvert text? Create two-column analysis showing text literally says X but image suggests Y. How does this tension create meaning? Would each element alone communicate differently?

Success Criteria: You can name two conventions of this text type and how this example uses or bends them.
If You Are Stuck: List what you'd EXPECT this text type to do. Then hunt for the one place this example does something different.

Activity Description: Select strongest piece of evidence from appeals. For this one element, write brief explanation at each level: Literal (surface), Figurative (hidden meaning), Technical (techniques creating effect), Thematic (connects to larger patterns), Conceptual (what reader understands), Effectual (impact on audience), Societal (broader implications). 150-200 words total.

Success Criteria: You can explain one sound or line-break choice as meaningful, not decorative.
If You Are Stuck: Read it aloud once. Circle where your voice naturally slowed. Analyse that spot.

Activity Description: Analyse poems structure systematically. Create outline showing: opening strategy, development arc, climactic moment, conclusion type. Does it follow conventional structure for this genre? What departures exist? How does structure guide reader/viewer interpretation? What would change if you reversed sections?
Helps With: Getting Started, Analysis Depth, Structure & Planning

Success Criteria: You can explain one sound or line-break choice as meaningful, not decorative.
If You Are Stuck: Read it aloud once. Circle where your voice naturally slowed. Analyse that spot.

Activity Description: For poems: read aloud or listen to audio version. Notice: sentence length variety (short/long pattern), sound patterns (alliteration, assonance), pauses and silence, punctuation rhythm, word choice musicality. Does rhythm accelerate or decelerate? How does sonic texture reflect content meaning?

Activity Title: The Tyger: Sound Under the Meaning
Activity Description: Read 'The Tyger' aloud twice. This poem runs on relentless interrogative structure and trochaic drumbeat rhythm. Tap the rhythm on the desk on the second read. Then write two sentences: where does the sound pattern hold steady, and where does it break or strain? What does that break make you feel about the unanswerable questions about creation and evil?
Materials: Printed Text or Screen, Pen & Paper
Why It Helps: Deepens your knowledge of 'The Tyger' for the IO — poem-level detail is what turns a general global issue into a convincing 10-minute argument.
Success Criteria: You can point to one line where sound and meaning pull in the same direction — and say it aloud.
If You Are Stuck: Read just the first stanza aloud in a whisper, then loudly. Which felt wrong? Why?

Activity Title: The Tyger: One Image, One Argument
Activity Description: Take the central image of 'The Tyger' — think of the surprisingly tame, almost smiling tiger Blake actually engraved. Write the image at the centre of a page. Around it, branch out: literal details → connotations → what it suggests about the unanswerable questions about creation and evil. Finish with one sentence beginning 'Through this image, Blake argues that…'
Why It Helps: Deepens your knowledge of 'The Tyger' for the IO — poem-level detail is what turns a general global issue into a convincing 10-minute argument.
Success Criteria: Your final sentence names an argument, not a topic — something a listener could disagree with.
If You Are Stuck: Describe the image to yourself as if to a young child. The word they'd ask about first is your way in.

Activity Title: The Tyger: Mirror Reading with The Lamb
Activity Description: Set 'The Tyger' beside 'The Lamb'. Draw a two-column table: same subject, two visions. Fill three rows — speaker's voice, one shared image, ending. Then answer: is the Experience version correcting the other poem, or grieving it? One paragraph.
Why It Helps: Blake's paired poems are built for comparison — exactly the linking skill the IO (and Paper 2) reward.
Success Criteria: Your paragraph holds both poems in the same sentences instead of describing them one after the other.
If You Are Stuck: Find one word or image that appears in BOTH poems. Compare only what surrounds it.

Activity Description: Set a timer for 60 seconds and pitch aloud: how does 'The Tyger' illuminate how societies explain the existence of violence and evil? You must quote or closely paraphrase once — 'Did he who made the Lamb make thee?' is a strong candidate — and end with '…and that is why this issue still matters.' Record it; listen once; note one improvement.
Why It Helps: The IO is spoken, not written — this builds the specific muscle of arguing from a poem out loud under time.
Success Criteria: You filled the full 60 seconds, used the quotation, and landed the closing line without reading.
If You Are Stuck: Do a 20-second version first: issue, quote, one sentence of why. Then stretch it.

Activity Description: Blake engraved his poems as illuminated plates. Look up the plate for 'The Tyger' — notice the surprisingly tame, almost smiling tiger Blake actually engraved. Write three sentences: what the image adds that the words never say, what it quietly contradicts, and why that gap matters for the unanswerable questions about creation and evil. This doubles as multimodal analysis practice.
Why It Helps: Blake's plates make him secretly multimodal — analysing text with image trains the same skill you need for visual texts everywhere.
Success Criteria: You found one genuine tension between what the plate shows and what the poem says.
If You Are Stuck: List three things IN the picture that are never mentioned in the poem. Pick the strangest.

Activity Description: Pretend 'The Tyger' just appeared in an exam and you've never met it. Give yourself 5 minutes to produce a Paper-1-style plan: guiding question ('How does the text use relentless interrogative structure to shape meaning?'), three technique points, one line of context-free evidence each. Notice how much faster planning is on a text you know — that speed is the goal for real unseens.
Why It Helps: Cross-trains IO knowledge into Paper 1 method: same analytical engine, different exam. One session, two assessments.
Success Criteria: A complete 3-point plan existed when the timer rang.
If You Are Stuck: Skip the guiding question. List any three techniques first; the question will emerge from what they share.

Activity Description: Find the poem in The World's Wife whose speaker would have the strongest opinion about 'The Tyger' — about the unanswerable questions about creation and evil. Write a short paragraph comparing how each poet handles voice: who speaks, who is spoken FOR, and one technique each. You are practising Paper 2 comparison while revising your IO text.
Why It Helps: Links your IO literary text to your Paper 2 poetry collection — double-duty revision through one comparison.
Success Criteria: You justified the pairing with a real shared concern, not just 'both are poems'.
If You Are Stuck: Default pairing: 'London' ↔ 'Mrs Sisyphus' (endless imposed labour), or your poem ↔ 'Little Red-Cap' (innocence meeting power).

Activity Description: Make a quick ledger for 'The Tyger': every repeated word, phrase, or structure down the left; tally marks in the middle; on the right, what changes (if anything) at each return. Repetition in Blake is never neutral — decide whether each repeat comforts, traps, or accuses, given that the poem is about the unanswerable questions about creation and evil.
Why It Helps: Deepens your knowledge of 'The Tyger' for the IO — poem-level detail is what turns a general global issue into a convincing 10-minute argument.
Success Criteria: Your ledger has at least three entries and each has a verdict: comforts, traps, or accuses.
If You Are Stuck: Start with 'and'. Blake uses it like a hammer. Count them in one stanza.

Why It Helps: Deepens your knowledge of 'Nurse's Song (Innocence)' for the IO — poem-level detail is what turns a general global issue into a convincing 10-minute argument.
Success Criteria: Your ledger has at least three entries and each has a verdict: comforts, traps, or accuses.
If You Are Stuck: Start with 'and'. Blake uses it like a hammer. Count them in one stanza.

Activity Description: Re-read 'Little Red-Cap'. The persona is a young woman narrating her own entry into — and exit from — an older poet's world. Track the voice across the poem: label each stanza with one word for its tone. Where exactly does the tone turn? Quote the turning line from your copy and write two sentences on what triggers the shift.
Why It Helps: Poem-level mastery of 'Little Red-Cap' — Paper 2 essays live or die on this kind of specific knowledge (Criterion A).
Success Criteria: You located the exact line where the voice changes — not just 'near the end'.
If You Are Stuck: Read only the first line and the last line. Name the difference; then hunt for where it happened.

Activity Description: 'Little Red-Cap' rewrites Little Red Riding Hood. In two columns, list what the source story emphasises versus what Duffy's version emphasises. Circle the single most significant change. Write three sentences: what the change is, why it matters for innocence, experience, and creative independence, and what it reveals about the collection's whole project.
Why It Helps: Poem-level mastery of 'Little Red-Cap' — Paper 2 essays live or die on this kind of specific knowledge (Criterion A).
Success Criteria: Your 'most significant change' is defended, not just asserted.
If You Are Stuck: Ask: in the original, who gets to speak? In Duffy's version, who speaks? Start there.

Activity Description: The engine of 'Little Red-Cap' is the volta from seduction to the axe. Find the two or three places in your copy where this technique is strongest. For each: quote the words, name the effect on the reader, and connect it to innocence, experience, and creative independence. Write it as three tight analytical sentences you could drop into an essay.

Success Criteria: Three sentences exist that you could reuse verbatim in a timed essay.
If You Are Stuck: Find the line that makes you smile or wince. The technique is usually hiding in it.

Activity Description: From memory first: describe the final image of walking out of the forest alone, flowers in hand in 'Little Red-Cap' — then check your copy and correct yourself. Success Criteria: Your from-memory version needed at most small corrections.

Activity Description: Pair 'Little Red-Cap' with a precise moment in A Doll's House: Nora walking out of the house at the end of Act Three. This pairing is exam ammunition — file it in your comparison bank.

For each: quote the words, name the effect on the reader, and connect it to maternal love and renewal. Write it as three tight analytical sentences you could drop into an essay.
Why It Helps: Ready-made Criterion B material: technique + quotation + effect, pre-drafted before exam day.
Success Criteria: Three sentences exist that you could reuse verbatim in a timed essay.
If You Are Stuck: Find the line that makes you smile or wince. The technique is usually hiding in it.

Activity Description: From memory first: describe the daughter bringing "all spring's flowers" in 'Demeter' — then check your copy and correct yourself. Write one sentence on why THIS moment (not another) is where the poem's meaning concentrates, and one on how you would use it to answer a question about maternal love and renewal.
Why It Helps: Testing recall BEFORE checking is the fastest way to find and fix knowledge gaps pre-exam.
Success Criteria: Your from-memory version needed at most small corrections.
If You Are Stuck: Open the book, read the moment once, close the book, and describe it aloud. That counts.

Activity Description: Pair 'Demeter' with a precise moment in A Doll's House: the play's children and what Nora leaves them for. Write a comparative paragraph with one grounds of comparison (maternal love and renewal), one technique from each text, and a 'While… whereas…' sentence holding both. This pairing is exam ammunition — file it in your comparison bank.
Text Type(s): The World's Wife, A Doll's House
Text / Work Focus: The World's Wife, A Doll's House
Why It Helps: Paper 2 is comparison under pressure — pre-built pairings like this are the single best preparation.
Success Criteria: Your paragraph could answer a real question on gender, power, or voice with both texts present throughout.
If You Are Stuck: Write the 'While…' sentence first, even badly. The paragraph grows around it.

Activity Description: Duffy's 'Demeter' is a dramatic monologue — a voice engineered for effect, like an opinion column or a first-person ad. Find any first-person non-literary text (column, confessional Instagram caption, charity appeal letter). Compare the voice-building toolkit: pronouns, register, direct address, self-revelation. Two sentences on each text; one on what 'constructed voice' means for BOTH Paper 1 and Paper 2.
Text Type(s): The World's Wife, Opinion Column, Social Media Post
Why It Helps: One skill, two papers: recognising a constructed first-person voice scores in Paper 1 unseen texts AND Paper 2 poetry.
Success Criteria: You can name two voice-building techniques the poem and the media text share.
If You Are Stuck: Count the 'I's in each text first. What is each 'I' trying to make you feel?

Activity Description: Copy out the first line of 'Demeter'. Interrogate it: what does it establish about speaker, tone, and situation before line two exists? What promise does it make that the ending keeps or breaks? Then the title: why 'Demeter' and not the husband's name alone? Four sentences total.
Why It Helps: Openings and titles are high-yield: examiners reward candidates who analyse how poems begin, not just what happens.
Success Criteria: You stated one thing the first line PROMISES — and tracked whether the poem keeps it.
If You Are Stuck: Rewrite the first line in the dullest possible words. What died? That's what to analyse.

Hunt for the row where they disagree, understate, or overshoot each other. That tension is where graphic memoirs make meaning. One sentence: what does the gap say about the physical reality behind the word "refugee"?
Why It Helps: Panel-level knowledge of The Boat Escape turns a vague IO point about refugees or family into precise, quotable visual analysis.
Success Criteria: You found one real word-image gap — not a spread where they simply agree.
If You Are Stuck: Look for a calm caption over a violent image, or a violent caption over a calm image. Bui does both.

Activity Title: The Boat Escape: Choose Its Gallery Partner
Activity Description: If you had to hang one Banksy, Kruger, or Gallenkuş work next to The Boat Escape in an exhibition about the physical reality behind the word "refugee", which would it be? Decide in 60 seconds, then defend for five: two sentences on the shared issue, two on the DIFFERENT toolkits (memoir brushwork vs stencil/typography/photo-composite), one on what the pairing reveals that neither shows alone.
Text / Work Focus: Thi Bui, Banksy, Barbara Kruger, Ugur Gallenkuş
Why It Helps: The IO's heart is a literary/non-literary pairing on one issue — this drills exactly that selection-and-defence move.
Success Criteria: Your defence names one thing ONLY the pairing reveals.
If You Are Stuck: Water panels pair naturally with Gallenkuş's sea-crossing composites; silence panels with Kruger's shouting text — try the contrast.

Activity Description: Open The Best We Could Do to Redrawn Family Photographs — by re-drawing rather than reprinting photographs, Bui claims interpretation over documentation. Choose the single strongest panel. Autopsy it: framing (close/wide?), line weight, use of the orange wash, text placement, gutter before and after. Write three sentences linking these choices to who owns and retells a family's history.
Why It Helps: Panel-level knowledge of Redrawn Family Photographs turns a vague IO point about refugees or family into precise, quotable visual analysis.
Success Criteria: You named at least three concrete visual choices — not just what the panel shows, but how.
If You Are Stuck: Ask of the panel: why THIS distance from the subject? Closer = intimacy or claustrophobia; further = loneliness or safety.

Activity Description: Look at Girl with Balloon — a stencilled girl reaching for a heart-shaped balloon, captioned "There is always hope" at its Southbank site. In 60 seconds, list every deliberate choice you can see: subject, placement, scale, colour, text (if any), what's excluded. Then circle the choice that carries the most meaning for hope and loss in public space and write two sentences on it.
Why It Helps: Work-level knowledge of Banksy — being able to name and analyse specific pieces like Girl with Balloon is what lifts an IO from general to convincing.
Success Criteria: Your list has at least six choices, and your circled one connects clearly to the issue.
If You Are Stuck: Start with the hook: whether the balloon is leaving her or being released. Everything radiates from there.

Activity Title: Girl with Balloon: Two-Minute IO Slice
Activity Description: Deliver a two-minute mini-IO segment on Girl with Balloon and hope and loss in public space, aloud, standing up. Structure: issue in one sentence → the work described in two → three analysed choices (stencil economy, placement, irony) → one sentence on wider relevance. Record it. Listening back, count your filler words — that number is your target to halve next time.
Why It Helps: Simulates a real IO segment at real scale — two minutes is roughly what one work gets in your ten.
Success Criteria: You hit all four structural beats inside two minutes without reading from notes.
If You Are Stuck: Do it once badly on purpose. The second take is always easier — you've already heard yourself survive it.

Activity Title: Girl with Balloon: Find Its Paper 1 Cousin
Activity Description: Girl with Balloon works through whether the balloon is leaving her or being released. Find a real non-literary text — an ad, a charity appeal, a news photo, a poster — that uses the same core technique. Put them side by side and write four sentences: technique in the artwork, technique in the found text, one difference in purpose, one sentence on how you'd analyse the found text in Paper 1.
Why It Helps: Your IO artist becomes a Paper 1 training set: the same visual grammar appears in exam texts constantly.
Success Criteria: The shared technique is named precisely (not 'both are powerful').
If You Are Stuck: Charity appeals borrow these tricks shamelessly. Search one from a major NGO and compare.

Activity Title: Girl with Balloon: The Literary Handshake
Activity Description: Pair Girl with Balloon with the strongest moment from your literary IO texts — a Blake poem or a Thi Bui chapter — for the issue of hope and loss in public space. Write the pairing as an IO thesis sentence: 'Both [literary text] and Banksy's Girl with Balloon expose…, but where one uses…, the other uses…'. Then list the two extract moments you'd bring.

Write the pairing as an IO thesis sentence: 'Both [literary text] and Ugur Gallenkuş's The Bedtime Pair expose…, but where one uses…, the other uses…'. Then list the two extract moments you'd bring.
Text / Work Focus: Ugur Gallenkuş, William Blake, Thi Bui
Why It Helps: The IO is exactly this handshake — one literary, one non-literary, one issue. Every rehearsal of it compounds.
Success Criteria: Your thesis sentence contains both texts, the issue, AND a difference in method.
If You Are Stuck: Template: 'Both X and Y expose [issue], but where X uses [technique], Y uses [technique].' Fill five blanks.

Activity Title: The Bedtime Pair: Move It and See
Activity Description: Thought experiment: relocate The Bedtime Pair — a duvet-wrapped sleeper completed by a child asleep on cardboard — into three new contexts: a gallery wall, an Instagram feed, a school corridor. For each, one sentence: what survives, what dies, what changes meaning? Conclude: how much of this work's power IS its context? That question impresses in the IO's discussion section.
Why It Helps: Context questions are favourite IO follow-ups — this preloads a thoughtful answer.
Success Criteria: You reached a verdict: context is essential / helpful / almost irrelevant for this work — with a reason.
If You Are Stuck: Start with the silliest move (the school corridor). The absurdity shows you what the work needs.

Activity Description: Look at The Hospital Pair — a monitored neonatal ward fused with an improvised clinic. In 60 seconds, list every deliberate choice you can see: subject, placement, scale, colour, text (if any), what's excluded. Then circle the choice that carries the most meaning for healthcare inequality at birth and write two sentences on it.
Why It Helps: Work-level knowledge of Ugur Gallenkuş — being able to name and analyse specific pieces like The Hospital Pair is what lifts an IO from general to convincing.
Success Criteria: Your list has at least six choices, and your circled one connects clearly to the issue.
If You Are Stuck: Start with the hook: machines on one side, hands on the other. Everything radiates from there.

Activity Title: The Hospital Pair: Two-Minute IO Slice
Activity Description: Deliver a two-minute mini-IO segment on The Hospital Pair and healthcare inequality at birth, aloud, standing up. Structure: issue in one sentence → the work described in two → three analysed choices (split composition, matching geometry, colour contrast) → one sentence on wider relevance. Record it. Listening back, count your filler words — that number is your target to halve next time.
Why It Helps: Simulates a real IO segment at real scale — two minutes is roughly what one work gets in your ten.
Success Criteria: You hit all four structural beats inside two minutes without reading from notes.
If You Are Stuck: Do it once badly on purpose. The second take is always easier — you've already heard yourself survive it.

Activity Title: The Hospital Pair: Find Its Paper 1 Cousin
Activity Description: The Hospital Pair works through machines on one side, hands on the other. Find a real non-literary text — an ad, a charity appeal, a news photo, a poster — that uses the same core technique. Put them side by side and write four sentences: technique in the artwork, technique in the found text, one difference in purpose, one sentence on how you'd analyse the found text in Paper 1.
Why It Helps: Your IO artist becomes a Paper 1 training set: the same visual grammar appears in exam texts constantly.
Success Criteria: The shared technique is named precisely (not 'both are powerful').
If You Are Stuck: Charity appeals borrow these tricks shamelessly. Search one from a major NGO and compare.

Activity Title: Narrow the Field: Culture, Identity and Community
Activity Description: The IB field 'Culture, Identity and Community' is too broad to BE your global issue — it's the neighbourhood, not the address. Brainstorm four specific issues inside it (think: belonging, language loss, diaspora, tradition versus change). For each, ask the two IB tests: is it significant on a wide/global scale? Is it visible in BOTH one of your literary texts and one body of work? Circle the one that passes best and draft it as a single sentence.
Why It Helps: A well-scoped global issue is the single biggest predictor of a coherent IO — everything else hangs from it.
Success Criteria: Your circled issue fits in one sentence and you can already name one moment per text for it.
If You Are Stuck: Use the frame: 'the way [group/system] [verbs] [group/thing]' — e.g. 'the way institutions justify child labour'.

Activity Title: Pairing Lab: William Blake × Banksy on Culture
Activity Description: Audition the pairing of a Blake poem with a Banksy work for an issue in the field of culture, identity and community (e.g. belonging). Fill a five-line audition card: the issue in one sentence / strongest extract moment from each / one technique each / what the PAIRING shows that neither text shows alone. Verdict out of 10 — keep a league table of your auditions.
Why It Helps: Choosing the right pairing IS the IO — auditioning pairs systematically beats settling for the first idea.
Success Criteria: The audition card's last line names something only the pairing reveals.
If You Are Stuck: Score each line of the card 0–2 as you go. A pairing scoring under 6 is telling you something.

Verdict out of 10 — keep a league table of your auditions.
Why It Helps: Choosing the right pairing IS the IO — auditioning pairs systematically beats settling for the first idea.
Success Criteria: The audition card's last line names something only the pairing reveals.
If You Are Stuck: Score each line of the card 0–2 as you go. A pairing scoring under 6 is telling you something.

Activity Description: Draft your IO skeleton on one page: ~4 minutes literary text + extract, ~4 minutes body of work + extract, ~2 minutes synthesis. Under each block write ONE thesis-bullet and TWO evidence-bullets. No sentences — bullets only. This page is your master map; every rehearsal starts from it.
Text / Work Focus: William Blake, Thi Bui, Banksy, Barbara Kruger, Ugur Gallenkuş
Why It Helps: The IO rewards balanced treatment — a timed blueprint prevents the classic error of spending seven minutes on the first text.
Success Criteria: Your skeleton fits one page and the two text blocks are visibly equal in weight.
If You Are Stuck: Steal this spine: issue → text 1 close reading → text 2 close reading → what they show together. Fill in bullets.

Activity Description: Deliver your full IO aloud with a visible timer: 10 minutes, no stopping, glance-notes only (10 bullet points maximum). Whatever goes wrong, keep going — recovering IS the skill. Afterwards note: where did you overrun? Which section felt thin? Fix only the worst section before your next run.
Text / Work Focus: William Blake, Thi Bui, Banksy, Barbara Kruger, Ugur Gallenkuş
Why It Helps: Nothing predicts IO performance like full-length timed rehearsals — each one you complete lowers exam-day adrenaline.
Success Criteria: You reached the synthesis section before 8:30 and finished by 10:00.
If You Are Stuck: Do a 5-minute half-scale version first: 2 min per text, 1 min synthesis. Then double it.

Activity Description: Take your candidate extract (max 40 lines or one visual work). Highlight in three colours: evidence for your global issue / striking technique / lines you'd cut if allowed. If the first two colours don't dominate, this is the wrong extract. Choose the 4–6 moments you will actually reference, and number them in speaking order.
Text / Work Focus: William Blake, Thi Bui, Banksy, Barbara Kruger, Ugur Gallenkuş
Why It Helps: Examiners reward candidates who navigate a deliberately chosen extract — not ones who gesture at a whole text.
Success Criteria: You have 4–6 numbered moments, each tagged with issue-relevance and a technique.
If You Are Stuck: Ask of every line: 'does this earn its place for MY issue?' Cut ruthlessly; what remains is your map.

Activity Description: Practise only your connective tissue: say aloud, from memory, your opening sentence, the bridge from text 1 to text 2, the pivot into synthesis, and your closing sentence. Four sentences, polished until automatic. These are the moments nerves attack first — armour them.
Text / Work Focus: William Blake, Thi Bui, Banksy, Barbara Kruger, Ugur Gallenkuş
Why It Helps: Criterion C is largely won in the joins — rehearsed transitions keep the argument visibly organised under pressure.
Success Criteria: You can deliver all four signposts back-to-back without notes.
If You Are Stuck: Write each signpost on one flashcard. Shuffle and test yourself out of order.

Activity Description: After your 10 minutes, your teacher asks questions for 5. Predict them: write eight questions you'd ask if you were the examiner (the mean ones too — 'isn't this issue too broad?', 'what would a critic say against your reading?'). Answer two aloud in full sentences. Repeat with two different questions tomorrow.
Text / Work Focus: William Blake, Thi Bui, Banksy, Barbara Kruger, Ugur Gallenkuş
Why It Helps: The discussion section is unscripted by design — but predicted questions convert most of it back into prepared ground.
Success Criteria: You answered without a silence longer than three seconds.
If You Are Stuck: Start every answer with 'That's fair — and…' or 'In the text itself…' — stems that buy thinking time.

Activity Description: You may bring ten bullet points. Build them: one bullet per structural beat, maximum six words each, no full sentences. Now rehearse two minutes of your IO using ONLY the card. If you read any bullet aloud verbatim, rewrite it shorter. The card is a trampoline, not a script.
Text / Work Focus: William Blake, Thi Bui, Banksy, Barbara Kruger, Ugur Gallenkuş
Why It Helps: Over-written notes cause reading-aloud — the fastest way to lose Criterion D marks for delivery.
Success Criteria: Every bullet is ≤6 words and triggers a paragraph of speech.
If You Are Stuck: Write the bullet as the QUESTION your paragraph answers ('why gutters?') rather than its summary.

Activity Description: Record any two-minute slice of your IO. Listen back once with the rubric beside you and score yourself honestly on all four criteria. Write one sentence per criterion: the single change that would raise it. Re-record the same slice applying only Criterion-with-lowest-score's fix.
Text / Work Focus: William Blake, Thi Bui, Banksy, Barbara Kruger, Ugur Gallenkuş
Why It Helps: Self-marking against the actual rubric converts vague worry into a specific to-do list.
Success Criteria: Your re-recording measurably improves the targeted criterion.
If You Are Stuck: Can't bear your own voice? Normal. Listen anyway — it fades by the third time, and the data is worth it.

Activity Description: Draft three different opening sentences for your IO: one starting from the global issue in the world today, one from a striking detail in a text, one from a question. Say each aloud. Keep the one that makes you want to continue — and note why it works: that's your rhetorical signature.
Text / Work Focus: William Blake, Thi Bui, Banksy, Barbara Kruger, Ugur Gallenkuş
Why It Helps: First impressions frame the whole assessment — a rehearsed, confident open settles you AND the examiner.
Success Criteria: One opening chosen, memorised, and deliverable while making eye contact.
If You Are Stuck: Borrow the shape 'In [text], [detail]. That detail is not small — it is [issue] in miniature.'

Activity Description: Take your latest full rehearsal (or plan) and audit the minutes: literary text / body of work / synthesis / anything else. Draw it as a bar chart. The IB expects roughly equal treatment of both texts — if one bar dwarfs the other, list two cuts and one addition to rebalance before your next run.
Text / Work Focus: William Blake, Thi Bui, Banksy, Barbara Kruger, Ugur Gallenkuş
Why It Helps: Unbalanced treatment is one of the most common IO penalties — and one of the easiest to fix with data.
Success Criteria: Your two text bars are within a minute of each other on paper.
If You Are Stuck: Can't cut anything? Move it to the Q&A ammunition list — nothing is wasted, it's just relocated.

Activity Description: Sixty seconds: list every visual-analysis term you can use in the IO (juxtaposition, framing, stencil, typography, colour palette, focal point, composition, negative space, direct address…). Circle the five you actually use when speaking. Tomorrow, deliberately use two NEW ones in rehearsal.
Text / Work Focus: William Blake, Thi Bui, Banksy, Barbara Kruger, Ugur Gallenkuş
Why It Helps: Criterion D rewards a precise analytical register — terms you can deploy aloud, not just recognise.
Success Criteria: Two new terms appeared naturally in your next rehearsal.
If You Are Stuck: Steal terms from photography tutorials: rule of thirds, leading lines, depth of field all transfer.

Activity Description: One technique, three arenas. Find juxtaposition in: (1) your IO texts — think Gallenkuş's split frames or Banksy's flower-throwing rioter; (2) a wild Paper 1 text — before/after ads, protest photography, charity appeals; (3) your Paper 2 works — innocent Nora against calculating Krogstad, or Duffy's myth-vs-wife framing. For each sighting write one analytical sentence: technique + evidence + effect. Note how the SAME move serves different purposes — that observation is transferable gold.
Why It Helps: Revises all three assessments in one sitting by making the technique — not the text — the unit of study.
Success Criteria: Three sentences, three different texts, one technique — each sentence names a DIFFERENT effect.
If You Are Stuck: Start with the given examples (Gallenkuş's split frames or Banksy's flower-throwing rioter). Your job is then just the Paper 1 wild find.

Activity Description: Write one masterclass paragraph defining juxtaposition and its typical effects, using your best single example from ANY of your texts (Gallenkuş's split frames or Banksy's flower-throwing rioter, or innocent Nora against calculating Krogstad, or Duffy's myth-vs-wife framing). Then list two 'adaptor sentences' showing how you'd redeploy the same paragraph in a Paper 1 response and a Paper 2 essay. You are building a reusable analytical asset.
Why It Helps: A bank of portable technique-paragraphs cuts exam thinking time dramatically — you adapt instead of inventing.
Success Criteria: The paragraph works standalone AND both adaptor sentences feel natural.
If You Are Stuck: Define the technique in your own words first, badly. Precision comes on the second pass.

Activity Description: Make one three-sided knowledge card for juxtaposition (use front / back / margin): FRONT — definition in ≤12 words. BACK — three examples: one IO (Gallenkuş's split frames or Banksy's flower-throwing rioter), one Paper 2 (innocent Nora against calculating Krogstad, or Duffy's myth-vs-wife framing), one Paper 1 text type where it thrives (before/after ads). MARGIN — two effect-verbs it usually triggers (unsettles, indicts, seduces…). Add to your deck; test in 48 hours.
Why It Helps: One card revises three assessments simultaneously — the highest-leverage flashcard you can own.
Success Criteria: The card exists, is legible, and the 48-hour self-test is scheduled.
If You Are Stuck: Copy the examples given in this activity. The forging is in the WRITING-DOWN, not the inventing.

Activity Title: Marriage as Performance: Build the Ladder
Activity Description: Build a five-rung evidence ladder for 'marriage as performance': rung 1 — define the theme in your own sentence; rungs 2–3 — one TWW moment (wives narrating the roles they played ('Mrs Midas', 'Pygmalion's Bride')) and one ADH moment (Nora's rehearsed girlishness and the tarantella), each with a micro-quotation; rung 4 — one technique per text; rung 5 — a thesis: what DIFFERENT verdicts do the two texts reach? Keep the ladder — it's a third of an essay plan.
Text Type(s): The World's Wife, A Doll's House
Text / Work Focus: The World's Wife, A Doll's House
Why It Helps: Paper 2 questions are theme-shaped — pre-built ladders mean you assemble essays instead of inventing them.
Success Criteria: Rung 5 names a genuine difference between the texts' verdicts, not just 'both explore it'.
If You Are Stuck: Rung 5 stem: 'While Duffy's wives [verb] this, Ibsen shows [character] [verbing]…'

Activity Title: Marriage as Performance: Five-Minute Plan Sprint
Activity Description: Invent a plausible exam question on marriage as performance (frame: 'In what ways and to what effect do writers of works you have studied…'). Then plan an answer in exactly five timed minutes: thesis, three comparative points (wives narrating the roles they played ('Mrs Midas', 'Pygmalion's Bride') vs Nora's rehearsed girlishness and the tarantella…), evidence tags. Stop when the timer stops. Rate your plan /10 for 'could I write from this?'
Text Type(s): The World's Wife, A Doll's House
Text / Work Focus: The World's Wife, A Doll's House
Why It Helps: The five-minute plan is Paper 2's highest-leverage habit — essays fail from unplanned drift more than weak knowledge.
Success Criteria: A stranger could see your three points and both texts in the plan.
If You Are Stuck: Always plan point 1 as the SIMILARITY and points 2–3 as escalating DIFFERENCES. It nearly always fits.

Activity Title: The Gaze and Objectification: Five-Minute Plan Sprint

Then plan an answer in exactly five timed minutes: thesis, three comparative points ('Medusa' weaponising being looked at vs Nora displayed, dressed, and choreographed by Torvald…), evidence tags. Stop when the timer stops. Rate your plan /10 for 'could I write from this?'
Text Type(s): The World's Wife, A Doll's House
Text / Work Focus: The World's Wife, A Doll's House
Why It Helps: The five-minute plan is Paper 2's highest-leverage habit — essays fail from unplanned drift more than weak knowledge.
Success Criteria: A stranger could see your three points and both texts in the plan.
If You Are Stuck: Always plan point 1 as the SIMILARITY and points 2–3 as escalating DIFFERENCES. It nearly always fits.

Activity Description: Decode five past-paper-style Paper 2 questions. For each, underline the instruction verb (discuss, compare, to what extent), circle the concept (identity, conflict, technique), and rewrite the question as your own one-line task statement. Speed matters more than beauty — this is the skill of never answering the wrong question.
Text Type(s): The World's Wife, A Doll's House
Text / Work Focus: The World's Wife, A Doll's House
Why It Helps: Misread questions sink more Paper 2 essays than any other cause.
Success Criteria: Five task statements, each mentioning both texts.
If You Are Stuck: The concept is usually the abstract noun. Find it, and ask: where do MY texts argue about this?

Activity Description: List ten possible 'grounds of comparison' between TWW and ADH — the shared territory a question might name: gender roles, power, voice, marriage, money, performance, freedom, justice, transformation, family. For each, jot the FIRST moment from each text that comes to mind. Gaps in your grid = revision priorities.
Text Type(s): The World's Wife, A Doll's House
Text / Work Focus: The World's Wife, A Doll's House
Why It Helps: A pre-mapped comparison grid converts any question into familiar ground.
Success Criteria: All ten rows have at least one moment per text.
If You Are Stuck: Do the five easiest rows first. Momentum makes the hard rows easier.

Activity Description: Write three Paper 2 introductions in 15 minutes using the formula: contextualising sentence naming both works and authors → direct answer to the question (thesis) → roadmap of your three points. Use three different themes. The formula should start feeling automatic — that's the point.
Text Type(s): The World's Wife, A Doll's House
Text / Work Focus: The World's Wife, A Doll's House
Why It Helps: An automatic introduction saves five exam minutes and sets up every later paragraph.
Success Criteria: Each intro is ≤5 sentences and states a real position.
If You Are Stuck: Bad first drafts allowed. The formula, repeated, does the polishing.

Activity Description: Upgrade your linking language: write one comparative paragraph on any theme using at least five different comparative connectives (whereas, conversely, similarly, more radically, while both). Underline them. Then delete the two weakest sentences — connectives should carry argument, not decorate it.
Text Type(s): The World's Wife, A Doll's House
Text / Work Focus: The World's Wife, A Doll's House
Why It Helps: Criterion C rewards visibly comparative structure — connectives are its load-bearing beams.
Success Criteria: Five distinct connectives doing real argumentative work.
If You Are Stuck: Ban 'both texts show' for one paragraph. Watch what you invent instead.

Activity Description: Write two conclusions for one practice question: version A summarises your points; version B answers 'so what?' — what do these two texts, together, reveal that neither could alone? Compare them. Version B is the one examiners remember; note the moves it made.
Text Type(s): The World's Wife, A Doll's House
Text / Work Focus: The World's Wife, A Doll's House
Why It Helps: Conclusions that synthesise (rather than repeat) push essays into the top bands.
Success Criteria: Version B says something not already in your body paragraphs.
If You Are Stuck: Stem: 'Read together, these works suggest that [theme] is ultimately…'

Activity Description: Take any three long quotations from your notes and slim each to under six words without losing the point. Then embed each slimmed quote in an analytical sentence. Long quotation is a Paper 2 disease — this is the cure.
Text Type(s): The World's Wife, A Doll's House
Text / Work Focus: The World's Wife, A Doll's House
Why It Helps: Short embedded quotations demonstrate command; long block quotes demonstrate memory only.
Success Criteria: Three sentences, each with a ≤6-word quotation inside it, grammar intact.
If You Are Stuck: Keep only the words you'd bold if you could. Cut the rest.

Activity Description: Sit a full Paper 2 under exam conditions: choose one question from a set you haven't planned, 105 minutes, handwritten, no notes. Then rest — mark it tomorrow against the criteria, not today. Schedule this no more than once a fortnight; it's a stress test, not a daily drill.
Text Type(s): The World's Wife, A Doll's House
Text / Work Focus: The World's Wife, A Doll's House
Why It Helps: Nothing else reveals your true exam behaviour — pacing, panic points, handwriting stamina — like the full sit.
Success Criteria: You finished a complete essay and can name your one biggest time-leak.
If You Are Stuck: If 105 minutes feels impossible, sit a half-paper (one body paragraph fewer) this week and the full one next week.

Activity Description: Take one of your practice paragraphs and the IB criteria. Highlight in four colours: knowledge claims (A), analysis of technique (B), structural signposts (C), precise vocabulary (D). Whichever colour is scarcest is your next session's focus. Marking yourself teaches the rubric faster than being marked.
Text Type(s): The World's Wife, A Doll's House
Text / Work Focus: The World's Wife, A Doll's House
Why It Helps: Internalising the rubric means writing FOR the marks in real time, not hoping afterwards.
Success Criteria: You identified your scarcest colour and named one concrete fix.
If You Are Stuck: No colour at all for B? Add one sentence starting 'The effect of this is…' — instant analysis.

Activity Description: Find any print or social ad for a product you'd never buy. Give yourself 30 silent seconds — no analysis, just looking/reading. Then close it and write from memory: purpose, audience, and the one feature that reached you first. Reopen and check. Paper 1 rewards trained first impressions; this protocol trains them.
Why It Helps: Speed-reading advertisements accurately is exactly what Paper 1's time pressure demands.
Success Criteria: Your from-memory purpose and audience survived the re-check.
If You Are Stuck: Just answer: who PAID for this text to exist, and what do they get if it works?

Activity Description: Draw a 2×2 bingo grid with the classic advertisement conventions: target audience, desire-creation, image-text anchor, call to action. Analyse your found example and tick each convention you can EVIDENCE (quote or describe the exact spot). Any un-ticked square is interesting: is the text bending its genre, and why? Two sentences on the most interesting square.
Why It Helps: Convention-awareness lets you praise conformity OR analyse deviation — either way you sound in command.
Success Criteria: Every tick has a pointed-to piece of evidence, not a vibe.
If You Are Stuck: Start with the give-away: what the ad is really selling (status? fear? belonging?).

Activity Description: Set 12 minutes. Write ONE polished analytical paragraph on your advertisement example, built as: claim about purpose → two evidenced techniques → effect on the target audience → link back to what the ad is really selling (status? fear? belonging?). Handwrite if your exam is handwritten. Then underline your two technique names — if you can't, that's tomorrow's vocabulary work.
Why It Helps: Paragraph-scale sprints build exam fitness without the cost of full essays — high reps, low dread.
Success Criteria: One complete paragraph inside the timer with two named techniques.
If You Are Stuck: Use the spine: 'This [text type] positions its audience to… It does this first through… and reinforces it with…'

Activity Description: Find TWO examples of advertisements aimed at different audiences (e.g. teen vs corporate, local vs global). Build a quick T-chart: register / imagery / structure / what's omitted. Write three sentences on how audience reshapes the same text type — this doubles as HL Paper 1 thinking, where two texts must be handled in one sitting.
Why It Helps: Seeing one text type flex across audiences deepens convention knowledge into genuine genre insight.
Success Criteria: Your T-chart shows at least three real differences with evidence.
If You Are Stuck: Same product, two brands. Same story, two outlets. Same city, two travel sites. Pick one.

Activity Description: Paper 1 arrives with a guiding question. Write three plausible ones for your advertisement example — one about target audience, one about audience positioning, one about what the ad is really selling (status? fear? belonging?). Choose the hardest and bullet a 3-point answer plan in five minutes. Inventing questions teaches you to see texts the way setters do.
Why It Helps: Students who can SET questions never freeze when handed one — the setter's perspective demystifies the paper.
Success Criteria: Three distinct questions and one workable plan exist.
If You Are Stuck: All guiding questions are cousins of: 'How does the text use [features] to [purpose]?' Fill the brackets three ways.

Activity Description: Copy these five starters onto a card you keep in your notes: 'The writer's choice of ___ creates…', 'This positions the reader to…', 'The effect is intensified by…', 'What appears to be ___ is actually…', 'By the end, this develops into…'. Now pick ANY text you know and complete all five about it. Yes, it feels mechanical. Mechanical is how fluency starts.
Support Level: Highly Scaffolded — steps are laid out for you
Why It Helps: When analysis won't come, borrowed sentence shapes carry you until your own arrive — every writer starts on rails.
Success Criteria: Five complete sentences exist about a real text.
If You Are Stuck: Do them out of order. Number 4 ('What appears to be…') is secretly the easiest.

Activity Description: Write one PETAL paragraph where each element is exactly one sentence, labelled in the margin: Point / Evidence / Technique / Analysis / Link. Use any text you know well. When it works, write the same paragraph WITHOUT labels and let the sentences join naturally. Two paragraphs, one skill: structure you can feel.
Support Level: Highly Scaffolded — steps are laid out for you
Why It Helps: Making the invisible skeleton visible once means you can trust it invisibly forever after.
Success Criteria: Both versions exist; the unlabelled one still has all five elements.
If You Are Stuck: Write E (the quotation) first. Point and the rest grow around evidence more easily than the reverse.

Activity Description: Find or ask your teacher for one high-scoring sample paragraph. Reverse-engineer it: number its sentences, then label each sentence's JOB (claims, quotes, analyses, links, zooms out). Copy the sequence of jobs — not the words — and write your own paragraph on a different text following the exact same job order.
Support Level: Highly Scaffolded — steps are laid out for you
Why It Helps: Imitating structure (never words) is the fastest legitimate shortcut to exam-grade writing.
Success Criteria: Your paragraph follows the model's job-sequence with your own content.
If You Are Stuck: No model available? Use this sequence: claim → context clause → quote → technique → effect → so-what.

Activity Description: Copy this frame and fill only the blanks: 'In this text, the creator uses [technique] in the line/image [evidence]. This makes the audience feel [emotion] because [reason]. This matters to the whole text because [link].' Complete it three times for three different techniques in one text. The frame is doing the structure; you're doing the thinking.
Support Level: Highly Scaffolded — steps are laid out for you
Why It Helps: Separating the thinking from the structuring lets a struggling writer practise one thing at a time.
Success Criteria: Three completed frames with three different techniques.
If You Are Stuck: Can't name the technique? Describe what the writer DID in plain words — 'repeated the word', 'made the image huge'. That counts.

Activity Description: Take one sentence you admire from any model essay or article. Copy it out exactly. Then rewrite it three times about YOUR texts, keeping the grammar skeleton but swapping the content. Example skeleton: 'What [X] presents as [A] is, on closer reading, [B].' This is how writers have always trained — theft of shape, never of substance.
Support Level: Highly Scaffolded — steps are laid out for you
Why It Helps: Sentence-level fluency is trainable through imitation — and Criterion D notices it immediately.
Success Criteria: Three new sentences share the skeleton but say true things about your texts.
If You Are Stuck: Choose a short sentence. Long ones hide their skeletons.

Activity Description: Rule a page into five rungs. Rung 1: write one true sentence about any text ('Blake wrote about children'). Rung 2: add a quoted word to it. Rung 3: add WHY the writer did it. Rung 4: add the effect on the reader. Rung 5: add 'which connects to the wider idea of…'. One sentence became analysis in five moves. You can always climb this ladder.
Support Level: Highly Scaffolded — steps are laid out for you
Why It Helps: Struggle usually means the first step is too big — this makes the first step laughably small, then compounds it.
Success Criteria: You reached rung 5 with one idea, however plain.
If You Are Stuck: Rung 1 can be embarrassingly obvious. 'The poem rhymes.' Climb from there — it works.

Activity Description: Can't start writing? Don't. Set a 2-minute timer and explain your text ALOUD to an empty chair (or a pet, or your phone's voice memo): what it is, what it's doing, the one clever thing you noticed. Then transcribe your best three spoken sentences. Speech unlocks what the blank page freezes.
Support Level: Highly Scaffolded — steps are laid out for you
Why It Helps: Most 'writing blocks' are really starting blocks — speech has a lower activation energy and produces usable sentences.
Success Criteria: Three transcribed sentences now exist on paper. That's a draft.
If You Are Stuck: Start the recording with 'OK so basically…' — informality is the door in.

Activity Title: One Question, One Text, One Minute
Activity Description: Pick any text within reach — an ad on your phone, a poem from class. Set 60 seconds. Answer ONE question in writing: 'What does this text want from me?' (attention? money? agreement? tears?). When the timer rings, stop mid-word if needed. You have just done textual analysis. It is genuinely that small a thing, repeated.
Support Level: Highly Scaffolded — steps are laid out for you
Why It Helps: Shrinks 'I can't analyse' to a 60-second act that is impossible to fail — repetition does the rest.
Success Criteria: Sixty seconds of writing happened.
If You Are Stuck: If even that feels big, answer aloud instead. Then once more, on paper.

Activity Description: Draw two columns. LEFT: how you'd describe a technique to a friend ('the ad makes you feel left out if you don't buy it'). RIGHT: translate to exam register ('the advertisement constructs exclusivity, positioning non-consumers as outsiders'). Do five rows. Keep both columns — the left one proves you understood; the right one is the same idea wearing a suit.
Support Level: Highly Scaffolded — steps are laid out for you
Why It Helps: You already have the ideas — this converts them into the register that scores, without pretending the plain version is worthless.
Success Criteria: Five translations, and you can read the right column aloud without cringing.
If You Are Stuck: Stuck translating? Upgrade only the verb first: makes → constructs, shows → exposes, uses → deploys.

Activity Description: Take one marked piece of work. Find the comment that stung most. Rewrite ONLY the sentence or section it refers to, applying the feedback. Compare before/after out loud. One improvement, fully banked, beats a page of vague resolutions. File the pair where you'll see it before the exam.
Support Level: Highly Scaffolded — steps are laid out for you
Why It Helps: Feedback becomes improvement only when re-drafted — this makes that transaction small and complete.
Success Criteria: A before/after pair exists and the after is visibly stronger.
If You Are Stuck: No marked work handy? Mark one of your own paragraphs against the criteria first, then fix the weakest sentence.

Activity Description: Find one excellent analytical paragraph (a model essay, a quality review, even this course's samples). Read it aloud twice — once for meaning, once listening only to its RHYTHM: where sentences are short, where they stack clauses. You are tuning your ear. Finish by writing one sentence that imitates the rhythm of your favourite sentence.
Support Level: Highly Scaffolded — steps are laid out for you
Why It Helps: Analytical register is caught as much as taught — the ear trains the hand.
Success Criteria: You can name one rhythm move the writer made (a short sentence after long ones counts).
If You Are Stuck: Use a film or music review from a broadsheet — analysis of art in confident prose is everywhere.

Activity Description: For when a text makes your mind white-out (practice now, deploy in exams): put your pen down. Breathe out longer than in, twice. Then write the dumbest true sentence about the text: 'This is an advertisement for shoes.' Then one more: who is it for? You are moving again. The ladder back to analysis is made of obvious sentences.
Support Level: Highly Scaffolded — steps are laid out for you
Why It Helps: Panic ends analysis; this is a rehearsed route back that starts from zero and cannot fail.
Success Criteria: You practised the full sequence once, calmly, so it's available when needed.
If You Are Stuck: There is no stuck here — 'I am looking at a text' is a legal first sentence.

Activity Description: Interrogate any text with this fixed list, one line each: Who made you? For whom? What do you want? What's your best trick? What are you hiding? What would your enemy say about you? Why now? Why this form? Answering even five of the eight generates more analytical material than staring ever will.
Support Level: Highly Scaffolded — steps are laid out for you
Why It Helps: Questions externalise analysis — instead of 'being insightful', you're just answering, which is easier.
Success Criteria: At least five answered lines that could seed paragraphs.
If You Are Stuck: Start with 'What do you want?' — every text wants something, and wanting is analysable.

Activity Description: Never finished a Paper 1 response? Start here. Step 1: read any short ad or poster (60s). Step 2: answer in writing — purpose? audience? (2 min). Step 3: find ONE technique and quote/describe it (2 min). Step 4: one sentence on its effect (2 min). Step 5: staple the four answers together with connectives. That IS a mini Paper 1 response. Do this daily with different text types.
Support Level: Highly Scaffolded — steps are laid out for you
Why It Helps: An on-ramp built of tiny, completable steps — because the hardest part of every assessment is the first honest attempt.
Success Criteria: You completed every step, however rough the product.
If You Are Stuck: Do only Step 1 today. Genuinely. Come back for Step 2 tomorrow — momentum compounds.

Activity Description: Overwhelmed by the essay? Shrink it. Step 1: pick ONE theme (say, control). Step 2: one TWW moment + one ADH moment for it, from memory or notes (3 min). Step 3: one sentence comparing them with 'While… whereas…' (2 min). Step 4: add one quoted word to each side. You now hold a complete comparative point — an essay is only four of these plus an intro. Repeat tomorrow with a new theme.
Text Type(s): The World's Wife, A Doll's House
Text / Work Focus: The World's Wife, A Doll's House
Support Level: Highly Scaffolded — steps are laid out for you
Why It Helps: An on-ramp built of tiny, completable steps — because the hardest part of every assessment is the first honest attempt.
Success Criteria: You completed every step, however rough the product.
If You Are Stuck: Do only Step 1 today. Genuinely. Come back for Step 2 tomorrow — momentum compounds.

Activity Description: IO feels impossible? Build the smallest version. Step 1: say your global issue aloud, any wording (30s). Step 2: name one Blake/Bui moment and one Banksy/Kruger/Gallenkuş work that show it (1 min). Step 3: speak for 60 seconds total — issue, text one, text two, done. That's the IO at 1:10 scale. Each week, double one section until you reach ten minutes.
Text / Work Focus: William Blake, Thi Bui, Banksy, Barbara Kruger, Ugur Gallenkuş
Support Level: Highly Scaffolded — steps are laid out for you
Why It Helps: An on-ramp built of tiny, completable steps — because the hardest part of every assessment is the first honest attempt.
Success Criteria: You completed every step, however rough the product.
If You Are Stuck: Do only Step 1 today. Genuinely. Come back for Step 2 tomorrow — momentum compounds.

Activity Description: Create the master document you'll thank yourself for: one page per studied work (TWW, ADH, Blake, Bui, each artist). Add three quotations/moments per page this week — each with location and one-word theme tags. Set a weekly 10-minute top-up. By exam season you'll own a personal anthology no cram session could build.
Why It Helps: Evidence banks built slowly are retained; ones built in May are rented.
Success Criteria: The document exists with all works represented.
If You Are Stuck: Start with the quotes already in your class notes — collection before curation.

Activity Description: Subscribe your eyes: pick three recurring non-literary sources (a columnist, a data-viz account, a campaign's ads) and skim them weekly with one analytical note each — 'noticed: tricolon in headline'. Ten minutes a week compounds into genre fluency no last-minute practice can fake. Set the weekly slot now.
Why It Helps: Paper 1 rewards accumulated genre exposure — a diet beats a binge.
Success Criteria: Three sources chosen and this week's three notes made.
If You Are Stuck: Steal sources: one broadsheet columnist, one NGO campaign, one brand you admire.

Activity Description: Start a running two-column log: LEFT — every mistake feedback identifies (vague thesis, floating quotes, no effects); RIGHT — the fix in your own words. Review the log before each practice piece. Mistakes repeated are habits; mistakes logged are curriculum.
Why It Helps: Your errors are the most personalised syllabus that will ever exist — logging turns them into lessons.
Success Criteria: The log exists with at least two entries.
If You Are Stuck: Mine your most recent marked work for entries one and two.

Activity Description: Three assessments, one calendar. Draw next week: assign each study day ONE lead assessment (P1 / P2 / IO) plus a 10-minute 'minor' from another. Rotation beats bingeing: spacing effects are real, and no assessment goes stale. Pin the plan where you study.
Why It Helps: Interleaved revision outperforms blocked cramming in retention studies again and again.
Success Criteria: Seven days, each with a lead and a minor, visible at your desk.
If You Are Stuck: Default rotation: P1 / P2 / IO / P1 / P2 / IO / rest.

Activity Description: Collect five practice questions for your target paper. Spend exactly four minutes per question planning an answer — thesis and three bullet points, then move on regardless. Five plans in twenty minutes. Planning fluency, not completeness, is the skill; full essays come later.
Why It Helps: High-rep planning removes the fear of the unseen question — you've dated its cousins.
Success Criteria: Five skeleton plans exist; the fifth was faster than the first.
If You Are Stuck: Recycle themes: power, voice, gender, money cover most questions between them.

Activity Description: Once a week, sit HALF an exam under real conditions: one Paper 1 text analysis, or one Paper 2 body section, or a 5-minute IO chunk delivered aloud. Full conditions, half length, weekly rhythm. Stamina builds without burnout, and Wednesday-you keeps exam-day-you honest.
Why It Helps: Weekly exposure at half scale inoculates against exam-day shock at a sustainable cost.
Success Criteria: This week's half-exam happened and is dated in your notes.
If You Are Stuck: Shrink further if needed: a quarter-exam beats a skipped one.

Activity Description: Distil each studied work onto a single page: five themes, eight micro-quotes, three techniques, one 'shape' diagram (the work's arc drawn as a line). No sentences — fragments and arrows only. The constraint forces prioritisation, which IS the revision. Four works, four pages, done across two days.
Why It Helps: Compression is retrieval practice in disguise — and the pages become your final-days review set.
Success Criteria: One complete page per work, readable at arm's length.
If You Are Stuck: Start from your quote bank and cut — curation is easier than creation.

Activity Description: Each morning this week: six micro-tasks in 30 minutes — one P1 first-impressions read, one technique flashcard run, one TWW/ADH comparative sentence, one IO signpost spoken aloud, one quote self-test, one past-question plan. Everything touched daily, nothing bingeed. Tick the grid; momentum is the metric.
Why It Helps: Daily contact with every assessment keeps all circuits warm without exhausting any of them.
Success Criteria: Six ticks today.
If You Are Stuck: Halve each task rather than skip it — three minutes of quotes still counts.

Activity Description: List every topic/work/skill for your next paper. Mark each: GREEN (secure), AMBER (shaky), RED (avoided). This week belongs to AMBER — highest return per hour. Schedule reds only if ambers finish. Greens get five-minute confirmations, not hours. Triage isn't giving up; it's aiming.
Why It Helps: Final-week hours are precious — spending them where marks actually move is a skill, not a compromise.
Success Criteria: Every topic has a colour and next week's slots follow the colours.
If You Are Stuck: Can't rate honestly? Rate by dread: mild dread = amber, strong dread = red.

Activity Description: Tonight is for consolidation, not conquest. 20 minutes maximum: re-read your one-pagers slowly, speak your IO signposts once, lay out tomorrow's logistics (route, ID, pens, water). Then close the books — genuinely. Sleep converts today's work into tomorrow's retrieval; all-nighters trade marks for anxiety.
Support Level: Highly Scaffolded — steps are laid out for you
Why It Helps: Sleep is the highest-yield revision activity that exists the night before — everything else is margin.
Success Criteria: Books closed by your set time; kit by the door.
If You Are Stuck: Anxious hands? Copy out your eight favourite micro-quotes once, beautifully, then stop.

Activity Description: Choose the eight quotations you most want available tomorrow (mix TWW, ADH, and IO texts if orals loom). Write each once from memory, check, correct. Do NOT expand the list tonight — eight secured beats thirty skimmed. End by saying your favourite aloud with its analytical point. Done. You are more ready than you feel.
Support Level: Highly Scaffolded — steps are laid out for you
Why It Helps: A small, secured evidence set carries further under pressure than a large, wobbly one.
Success Criteria: Eight from-memory attempts, all corrected.
If You Are Stuck: Pick short ones. 'Little skylark' is two words and fully weaponised.

Activity Description: Write a six-line note to exam-day you: your thesis formula, your paragraph spine, your two rescue starters ('The dumbest true sentence…'), and one line of perspective ('You have done this in practice N times'). Read it with breakfast. Externalised calm survives adrenaline better than remembered calm.
Support Level: Highly Scaffolded — steps are laid out for you
Why It Helps: A pre-written protocol is what you'll actually follow when nerves arrive — decide tonight so you don't debate tomorrow.
Success Criteria: The note exists and sits with your exam kit.
If You Are Stuck: Copy the formulas from this app's scaffold activities — tonight is assembly, not authorship.

Activity Description: End your session by writing: THREE things you now know/can do that you didn't an hour ago, TWO questions still open, ONE thing you'll start with next session. Ninety seconds, no polish. You've just built your own next lesson plan and proved the session mattered.
Support Level: Highly Scaffolded — steps are laid out for you
Why It Helps: How a session ends decides how much of it survives — two minutes of consolidation protects sixty of work.
Success Criteria: It happened before the books closed, not 'later'.
If You Are Stuck: Do the shortest version: one sentence, out loud, about what you did. That still counts.

Activity Description: Explain the main thing you just studied to the wall (or a sibling, or the dog) in under two minutes, no notes. Where you hesitate is where the gap is — write that gap down as next session's first task. Teaching is the fastest audit of learning.
Support Level: Highly Scaffolded — steps are laid out for you
Why It Helps: How a session ends decides how much of it survives — two minutes of consolidation protects sixty of work.
Success Criteria: It happened before the books closed, not 'later'.

Activity Description: Before closing your books: add today's one mistake or weakness to your error log, with its fix in your own words. One entry per session, every session. The log becomes your personalised mark-scheme over time.
Support Level: Highly Scaffolded — steps are laid out for you
Why It Helps: How a session ends decides how much of it survives — two minutes of consolidation protects sixty of work.

Activity Description: Copy out — by hand, nicely — the single best sentence YOU wrote today. Date it. Keep them all on one page. On rough days, that page is evidence: you can do this, in your own handwriting, repeatedly.

Activity Description: Memory needs echoes: put two calendar reminders for today's material — one in 48 hours ('re-test Blake quotes, 5 min'), one next week. Thirty seconds of scheduling doubles the value of the hour you just spent. Then close everything guilt-free.
Support Level: Highly Scaffolded — steps are laid out for you
Why It Helps: How a session ends decides how much of it survives — two minutes of consolidation protects sixty of work.

Activity Description: Take one breath and summarise today's text or topic aloud before you stand up — whatever fits in a single exhale. The compression finds your core understanding. If nothing fits in a breath, note that honestly: tomorrow starts with re-reading, and that's fine.

Activity Description: Scan today's margin notes for every '?', 'check this', or half-thought. Either resolve each in one line now, or copy it onto tomorrow's list. Unclosed loops leak attention all evening; this drains the buffer in five minutes.
Support Level: Highly Scaffolded — steps are laid out for you
Why It Helps: How a session ends decides how much of it survives — two minutes of consolidation protects sixty of work.

Activity Description: Two columns, one minute each: 'What felt hard today' and 'What I did anyway'. The second column is the one that predicts your results. Read it back once. Sessions end better on evidence than on vibes.
Support Level: Highly Scaffolded — steps are laid out for you
Why It Helps: How a session ends decides how much of it survives — two minutes of consolidation protects sixty of work.
Success Criteria: It happened before the books closed, not 'later'.
If You Are Stuck: Do the shortest version: one sentence, out loud, about what you did. That still counts.

Activity Description: Face a partner. Serve a micro-quotation from TWW, ADH, Blake, or Bui; they must return the work, speaker/context, and one analytical point — then serve back. Dropped return = point to server. First to five. Loud, fast, and better than flashcards for exam recall.
Text Type(s): The World's Wife, A Doll's House
Materials: Pen & Paper, Printed Text or Screen
Why It Helps: Studying with others adds retrieval pressure, instant feedback, and accountability that solo sessions can't fake.
Success Criteria: Everyone produced or performed something — nobody only watched.
If You Are Stuck: Halve the group or halve the time. Small and real beats big and theoretical.

Activity Description: Each write a timed paragraph on the same text (10 min). Swap. Underline the ONE sentence in your partner's work you wish you'd written, and say why. Rewrite your own paragraph stealing that move (not the words). Two writers, four improvements, twenty-five minutes.
Materials: Pen & Paper, Printed Text or Screen
Why It Helps: Studying with others adds retrieval pressure, instant feedback, and accountability that solo sessions can't fake.
Success Criteria: Everyone produced or performed something — nobody only watched.
If You Are Stuck: Halve the group or halve the time. Small and real beats big and theoretical.

Activity Description: One delivers any 3-minute IO slice; the other plays examiner with three prepared follow-up questions (one friendly, one probing, one mean-but-fair). Swap roles. Debrief with the rubric between you: one criterion-linked compliment and one fix each. The examiner role teaches as much as the candidate role.
Materials: Pen & Paper, Printed Text or Screen
Why It Helps: Studying with others adds retrieval pressure, instant feedback, and accountability that solo sessions can't fake.
Success Criteria: Everyone produced or performed something — nobody only watched.
If You Are Stuck: Halve the group or halve the time. Small and real beats big and theoretical.

Activity Description: Both analyse one Paper-1-style text for ten minutes — but one of you may only discuss VERBAL choices, the other only VISUAL/STRUCTURAL ones. Present to each other; then write one joint thesis that needs both lenses. This is how multimodal analysis is supposed to feel.
Materials: Pen & Paper, Printed Text or Screen
Why It Helps: Studying with others adds retrieval pressure, instant feedback, and accountability that solo sessions can't fake.
Success Criteria: Everyone produced or performed something — nobody only watched.
If You Are Stuck: Halve the group or halve the time. Small and real beats big and theoretical.

Activity Description: Partner A states their IO global issue and pairing. Partner B asks only questions — no advice — for five minutes: 'why this work and not another?', 'where exactly in the text?', 'who disagrees?'. Swap. Questions expose gaps gently; answers rehearse the real Q&A.
Materials: Pen & Paper, Printed Text or Screen
Why It Helps: Studying with others adds retrieval pressure, instant feedback, and accountability that solo sessions can't fake.
Success Criteria: Everyone produced or performed something — nobody only watched.
If You Are Stuck: Halve the group or halve the time. Small and real beats big and theoretical.

Activity Description: Groups of 3–5, one auctioneer. Auctioneer names a technique (juxtaposition, direct address, motif…). Teams bid by naming texts that use it — any studied work or wild text — and must justify each bid in one sentence. Weak justification = bid void. Most valid bids after ten techniques wins. Chaos, laughter, and a revision map of the whole course.
Materials: Pen & Paper, Printed Text or Screen
Why It Helps: Studying with others adds retrieval pressure, instant feedback, and accountability that solo sessions can't fake.
Success Criteria: Everyone produced or performed something — nobody only watched.
If You Are Stuck: Halve the group or halve the time. Small and real beats big and theoretical.

Activity Description: Circle up. Inner pair discusses a Paper 2 question everyone avoided in practice; outer circle tracks every move on paper: claims, evidence, comparisons, drift. Rotate pairs every four minutes. End by assembling the best plan from all rotations on the board. The avoided question is now everyone's strongest.

Activity Title: Gallery Walk: One Issue, Five Works
Activity Description: Post five texts around the room (a Blake poem, a Bui spread, one work each from Banksy, Kruger, Gallenkuş). Groups rotate with one shared global issue, leaving one sticky-note observation per station — no repeats allowed, so later groups must dig deeper. Final huddle: which TWO works make the strongest IO pairing for this issue, and why?
Materials: Pen & Paper, Printed Text or Screen
Why It Helps: Studying with others adds retrieval pressure, instant feedback, and accountability that solo sessions can't fake.
Success Criteria: Everyone produced or performed something — nobody only watched.
